messages.content: You are a viral short-form video creator coach with deep expertise in YouTube Shorts storytelling, analytics, video feedback, and content strategy. You speak from real creator experience — direct, no fluff.

Top comments from a @t3ssel8r video:

[1] (256 👍) "As an artist, but not a programmer, I would love to use this engine one day. Good luck with  the game, excited to see how it turns out."
[2] (114 👍) "Seriously, start a Patreon and I’ll buy this game engine."
[3] (79 👍) "​ @maximcherstvi9877  thanks, I appreciate the sentiment. Like you said, it's not worth it to me right now to take on the responsibility of delivering updates and exclusives to patreons. I have a well-paying real job, which means significant deviations from my main project (the game) are a bad investment of my time right now. However, I can't hold any ill will toward people who want to support what I'm doing. If nothing else, it's a good gauge of interest for a potential market opportunity in the future."
[4] (73 👍) "UGH it's so good. Your work is so good. So so so so good."
[5] (26 👍) "@slugintub  I believe they were referring to the Unity based pixel art game engine this creator has made for creating such effects and scenes. As in the rendering."
[6] (20 👍) "lmao he is doing unique thing which can be paid more than for 100k or even 1kk by studio and you think that he is just sell it for 5 bucks per month? omg. Even then, if not to sell he can make his own game which bring more money to him for his development, and it will be more pleasure giving, just than "$5 patreons" why always think "I paid him five bucks he has to work and do what I want 24/7". Moreover, this technology will give the author a very powerful advantage over competitors. Comments like yours have always been a trivial display of disrespect for other people's work. It's far worse than asking for something for free. You're just demeaning person labor and hardwork over intellectual property that's worth a lot more than even $5000."
[7] (18 👍) "likewise! your process videos are so impressive, and inspiring. I wish you had made your fire tutorial before I started working on my fire system haha."
[8] (17 👍) "Looking great! Even if I don't comment much, I'm always excited to see a new video about this. :)"
[9] (15 👍) "The engine's called unity"
[10] (13 👍) "It's a custom renderer built on Unity"
[11] (12 👍) "For real. I wish I could subscribe and get friends to support this. The technology is amazing."
[12] (10 👍) "It looks great! I think this is really impressive! I would love to know how to do something like this!"
[13] (9 👍) "I wish there was a way to get our hands on at least the alpha, be a tester, or something similar.
I don't even mind paying to test this."
[14] (8 👍) "Watched all of your videos, they are so inspiring that I'm learning how to make shaders in unity. Care to share how you did the edges of your meshes look like that? I love how the nearest edges look brighter."
[15] (7 👍) "@drewplunkett8501  it's not engine."
[16] (7 👍) "thanks! it is just some edge detection on the depth and normals of the viewport, and then shading based on the light. The details are confusing and not so interesting to explain, so I encourage you to try for yourself :)"
[17] (7 👍) "I've considered it (and it's been a popular request) but I know I won't be able to do a good job maintaining it and providing support while also working on my own game, so I've shelved the idea for now."
[18] (6 👍) "@t3ssel8r  What you could do is just make a patreon for others, who are willing to, to support you, even without any extras they get."
[19] (5 👍) "This is not a game engine."
[20] (5 👍) "time permitting, but for now my focus is on making the game"
[21] (4 👍) "@slugintub  yes but they mean the custom renderer he made for unity"
[22] (4 👍) "Start a patreon or kickstarter sometime soon. I think people would flock to this like crazy. Judging by your views/likes/comments alone you can see a marked rise in interest and eyes on this in the past 4 months. I'd give money to support development of this engine and this game right now. 

I don't even want to buy the engine (though I'd buy the game twice over), I just want to see how far you can take all of this. This is truly something special and breathtaking. This could be industry changing. I see so much potential here in terms of art style, gameplay, the engine itself.

Once you release this game, whatever it may be, be prepared for indie developers to flock to this and try to emulate/incorporate it. It'll be BOTW syndrome all over again. I hope you realize the massive potential this has. I will be watching your career with great interest."
[23] (4 👍) "@GymCritical  you know damn well that’s not what they are talking about…"
[24] (4 👍) "Watching your videos makes me want to cry, everything is so beautiful"
[25] (3 👍) "This project is going places. Nice, cozy places!"

Top comments from a @t3ssel8r video:

[1] (565 👍) "I've never seen a 3d pixel art game look this good, probably because I could tell that all that was done was putting pixel art textures on low poly models. But THIS?! This looks like a SNES actually got possessed by an angel."
[2] (232 👍) "This is a killer art style, when the camera moved, it was honestly like magic. Well done."
[3] (132 👍) "I feel pretty lucky to just have stumbled upon this considering how little views this has. You should really consider sharing this around. It's really inspiring!"
[4] (60 👍) "I will buy whatever this game becomes."
[5] (42 👍) "This is beautiful. I hope you are going to release your game one day."
[6] (35 👍) "YES!, I wan't this! I've been researching the possibilities of doing something like this for a couple of months. this is were pixel art needs to go"
[7] (24 👍) "Oh man. That rotation is even more impressive to look at, you should do that on every video showing your engine. Nice job!"
[8] (12 👍) "@rajbir.soc0  Did you read the comment? They said that it WASN'T like putting pixel art textures on low poly models."
[9] (9 👍) "incredible!"
[10] (9 👍) "Please give us a little inside to how you do this"
[11] (8 👍) "​ @mekrou5982  I think it would depend on your background. I'm pretty comfortable with graphics programming, so my approach was to just look at a lot of pixel art artworks and tutorials, and try to automate their process on the GPU. I personally learned a lot from demosceners like Inigo Quilez and the articles of catlike coding."
[12] (7 👍) "Everything about this is super awesome!!!!!! Those shadows 👌🏼👌🏼 those rain particles 👌🏼👌🏼👌🏼 and of course The Godrays 👌🏼👌🏼👌🏼👌🏼👌🏼. Keep it up! Great job!!"
[13] (6 👍) "Me too, it's beautiful!"
[14] (6 👍) "This is what I dream of a new FF Tactics game to look like. This is amazing."
[15] (6 👍) "This is honest to god the most GORGEOUS pixel art style I've ever seen. And the fact that the textures are procedurally generated makes it even more awesome!"
[16] (6 👍) "i just noticed that you uploaded this yesterday lol nice. im looking for ideas like this and doing research. good job!"
[17] (6 👍) "Absolutely gorgeous! Xenogears instantly came to mind! ^-^"
[18] (6 👍) "I am enamoured by the grass and tree leaves. Everything here looks amazing, but that in particular, is inspiring me so much to learn shaders for myself, and figure out how you did this. Keep up the great work, and let me know if you ever make this public, or care to share a bit of how this is done to set me off in the right direction!"
[19] (6 👍) "Are you using the ProPixelizer asset ?"
[20] (5 👍) "it's beautiful, that style seems wonderful to me <3"
[21] (5 👍) "I'm using my own pixel art shaders which suit my workflow a bit better. Lots of shared ideas with Elliot's ProPixelizer though!"
[22] (5 👍) "@t3ssel8r  Thank you so much for replying. I'm going to deep dive research into shaders as I never knew it was something I always wanted. Thank you so much for those resources. Good luck with this project. (If it's a video game- I can't wait to see your success.)"
[23] (5 👍) "What a great answer. I’d be curious if you ever decide to put something up like catlike coding does about this topic, I’m sure many people would be fascinated to hear."
[24] (4 👍) "@t3ssel8r  How would someone learn about creating something like this? I am a beginner in Unity and would love it if you could shed some light as I am so lost on how something like this was made."
[25] (4 👍) "what game engine is this?"

Top comments from a @t3ssel8r video:

[1] (172 👍) "the dislikes are people who want to replicate it but don't know how"

[3] (76 👍) "Pixel 'Breath of the Wild', let's go. Great work dude. :)"
[4] (18 👍) "You probably get a lot of comments like this, but I really wanted a "tutorial-like" video of how you achieve such marvelous results (of course, considering you have the resources and time to make such videos)

Nontheless, this is one of the most beautiful styles of game graphics I have ever seen. Keep on the good work."
[5] (13 👍) "I wish I know how to do those, but gave it a thumbs up, anyway."
[6] (8 👍) "This is really amazing stuff man!! If we end up using your style as inspiration for our game, your name and game will definitely go high up in the special thanks part of the credits. This is just absolutely beautiful!!"
[7] (8 👍) "THIS IS INSANE. ive been googling something like this for years. TAKE MY MONEY"
[8] (7 👍) "T3ss! You're killin it! Did you try versions of the fire using flat color? or with less noise? I've shown your work to a lot of people. Can't wait to see more"
[9] (5 👍) "Dude this is amazing!"
[10] (5 👍) "Stop him, he's too powerful! Very exciting work, and would love if you posted more. I think about these new videos all the time."
[11] (4 👍) "Amazing work! Perhaps a playlist of all the pixel rendering engine would make for better consumption. I want to binge all the videos :p"
[12] (3 👍) "Wow this is so cool!"

[14] (3 👍) "Can you make tutorials too? Looks amazing"
[15] (2 👍) "Dude, I wanna play that... SO MUCH!"
[16] (2 👍) "yeah, it's vertex shader billboards, similar to the unity terrain grass shader."
[17] (2 👍) "Is it possible to buy your engine from anywhere? Love your work! 👍🏻"
[18] (2 👍) "thanks, things are still in development and I am primarily building the backend for my own game. I don't have the resources to be able to support external users right now, but if the situation changes in the future I will consider it."
[19] (2 👍) "thanks, I would love to post more but sometimes I work on things that aren't so interesting to post yet"

[21] (2 👍) "yup"

You are a viral short-form video creator coach with deep expertise in YouTube Shorts storytelling, analytics, video feedback, and content strategy. You speak from real creator experience — direct, no fluff.
Write a short-form video script in the style of @t3ssel8r (Pixel Story).

In order to render a large amount of lights in-scene with a minimal impact to frame time a cheap deferred shading technique is used implemented here as a unity shader on a bounding volume In the material settings, the artist can control the light blending as well as the blending and size of a halo quantization for both the lighting and halo are separately controllable highlights for the pixel art outline can also be modulated with a slider On more powerful systems, screenspace shadows can be enabled, which raymarch the depth map to estimate shadowing Applying this material to a Unity particle system produces per-particle lighting with all of the usual optimizations that come with Unity particle rendering This setup was used to render the fireflies in this scene Thanks for watching

You are a viral short-form video creator coach with deep expertise in YouTube Shorts storytelling, analytics, video feedback, and content strategy. You speak from real creator experience — direct, no fluff.

Short-form video transcript from @t3ssel8r (pixel story niche):

Title: "Dynamic 3D Pixel Art Particle Lighting"

TRANSCRIPT:
In order to render a large amount of lights in-scene with a minimal impact to frame time a cheap deferred shading technique is used implemented here as a unity shader on a bounding volume In the material settings, the artist can control the light blending as well as the blending and size of a halo quantization for both the lighting and halo are separately controllable highlights for the pixel art outline can also be modulated with a slider On more powerful systems, screenspace shadows can be enabled, which raymarch the depth map to estimate shadowing Applying this material to a Unity particle system produces per-particle lighting with all of the usual optimizations that come with Unity particle rendering This setup was used to render the fireflies in this scene Thanks for watching

What did the audience respond to most?

Top comments (30 total):

[1] (434 👍) "remind us to buy your game when it comes out!"

In order to render a large amount of lights in-scene with a minimal impact to frame time a cheap deferred shading technique is used implemented here as a unity shader on a bounding volume In the material settings, the artist can control the light blending as well as the blending and size of a halo quantization for both the lighting and halo are separately controllable highlights for the pixel art outline can also be modulated with a slider On more powerful systems, screenspace shadows can be enabled, which raymarch the depth map to estimate shadowing Applying this material to a Unity particle system produces per-particle lighting with all of the usual optimizations that come with Unity particle rendering This setup was used to render the fireflies in this scene Thanks for watching

Top comments from a @t3ssel8r video:

[1] (39 👍) "Greetings from Sweden! Btw, this is great! Would love a tutorial:)"
[2] (28 👍) "I was wondering about your approach to pixelating the 3d models. I read up on Elliot Bentine's article on the topic, and implemented my own version based on his post-processing/dithering strategy. Do you use a similar method, or an entirely different approach? I would love to know, your visuals are so dang cool!"
[3] (20 👍) "I recreated this pretty closely and I used true GPU instancing with DrawMeshInstancedIndriect. I made a quad mesh with it's origin at the bottom center for correct billboarding, and applied a grass texture to it. When spawning the grass on startup, I read the terrains normals and heights at different points (like mentioned in the description) with the functions from TerrainData and pass them into the compute buffer. It works great with barely any framerate impact below 100k quads.

DrawMeshInstancedIndirect does not have frustum culling. So if you want large terrains you probably have to implement that or load the grass in chunks or something like that."
[4] (16 👍) "I just render to a low resolution RT. Elliot's method is more suited to games that have a mix of different resolution assets."
[5] (13 👍) "it's tricky to get right and there are many different things that affect it, including pixellation of entity movement, pixellation of camera movement, sub-pixel shifting of camera viewport, and of course pixel size.

If you need individual sprites to slide across each other smoothly at different speeds, then of course you have no choice but pixellate separately."
[6] (13 👍) "there's a few different workable ideas. particle systems are one way, although you will want to pause the particle system so that they are not being recomputed every frame. You can also try using GPU instancing, or using the builtin terrain details system (which also uses GPU instancing). Since each mesh is so simple, I've also had success just pre-baking chunks into meshes."
[7] (11 👍) "This looks amazing! Would you mind talking about how you were able to instantiate so many grass sprites? It looks like they're packed together super densely. Is it a particle system, VFX graph or something? Using a particle system with GPU instancing turned on, I'm able to instantiate only ~100k sprites (quads) before the game starts choking, and the environment feels sparse. Really curious how you managed this. 

Keep up the good work!"
[8] (11 👍) "the grass is made up of a bunch of grass-shaped sprites that are shaded with a single color equal to the color of the pixel at its base"
[9] (5 👍) "This is awesome!"
[10] (5 👍) "@t3ssel8r  is there a separate rt for each model, or is this for the entire scene?"
[11] (5 👍) "whole scene"
[12] (5 👍) "@t3ssel8r  I see! Thanks for the help- Really looking forward to seeing more of your videos!"
[13] (4 👍) "@t3ssel8r  I tried something similar at first, but found that locking objects to only moving pixel units at a time made them appear jittery, while not locking them made pixels swim. Did you find a way to get around this?"
[14] (4 👍) "Hi, I really love all your videos, nice stuff! I've got a question about the grass receiving shadows if thats ok? I'm trying something similar and using the built-in pipeline, and passing terrain position, and normals for each instance through a buffer to the shader. How are you using the world terrain position to calculate the shadow colour for the whole sprite? My understanding is that in the ForwardBase pass that the main directional light is calculated in, there isn't a way to pass in a world position into any of the unity macros to calculate shadow attenuation - so just wondering how you managed to achieved this?"
[15] (4 👍) "hey love your work! I was wondering how you created the lighting effect. Is it a custom shader or how did you achieve this effect?"
[16] (3 👍) "shadow maps. maybe the bug in UE is with the calculation of the shadow view matrix. I don't know much about UE but you could compare a renderdoc capture of a perspective camera and an ortho camera to see what's different"
[17] (3 👍) "it's essentially that, plus small adjustments to camera and viewport to make camera movements smooth"
[18] (2 👍) "such a nice grass shader... been tryin to render something like that on blender for a while hahahaha"
[19] (2 👍) "@t3ssel8r  I just want to say thanks for taking the time to respond to all my questions!

If you're not annoyed at this point, could you tell me where in your custom SRP you call Graphics.Blit?"
[20] (2 👍) "Hello! I've been trying to make a similar pixel art shader in Unreal Engine, with deferred rendering. The issue with UE is a bug that orthographic camera won't render shadows. I was thinking about some custom shadow solution. What kind of shadows are you using? Is this the built in system for unity? Do you have anything in mind that could help me? Even a few keywords would help a lot to point me to the right direction."

The boss ripped the paper and said, "This report is organized terribly. Do it again. Your job is on the line." It was the sixth rejection and the employee was freaking out about losing his job. So, he got an idea. The employee submitted a new report, but this one was special. The boss looked at it and shouted, "This is the worst one yet. The formatting is awful." Title: "sneaky voxel engine heightmap render distance trick"

TRANSCRIPT:
my boxer game has a pesky little nine chunks vendor distance but I about to see mountains much further away than that to increase the render distance without exploding my computer I had to do a sneaky little trick for distance height map approximation Step 1 spawn a massive subdivided mesh step two Loop through the vertices of this mesh take the X in set position and Sample the voxel world for its height and just set the Y position to that height now I can see Mount this much further away of course this is a huge approximation so it looks a bit wonky and it bleeds it to the terrain oh no the closer to the player we are I'm just gonna push the heights down a bit now it is perfect foreign

[1] (67 👍) "You could increase the render distance even further if you use GPU sided indirect rendering. I managed to get a volume of 12^3 chunks with a resolution of 64^2 running fine on .y laptop gtx 1050 due to the inherently parallel architecture of GPUs. I made it within my custom wgpu engine though, not really sure if bevy has support for GPU indirect drawing."
[2] (15 👍) "i love tantans accent"
[3] (8 👍) "Sorry to backseat but just a thought, couldn’t you occlusion cull valleys and rear side of hills using by checking the player position against the height map? In the simplest case, the player would be in a valley where the region is dark bordered by a light gradient so you could cull all regions that are darker than the surrounding lightest points of your boundary."
[4] (7 👍) "Since all the voxels align to a fixed grid, you could write a shader that converts the world position of every pixel to a block position, calculates the side of the voxel, and then sets the normal according to that side of the block.
That way, the terrain continues to look blocky from a distance.

In addition to generating a height-map texture, you could also create a BlockID-map texture to sample from so you can use the textures of the blocks to draw them out, making them seem more like the actual underlying terrain (minus a lot of detail).

Finally, you could adjust the draw order so that the actually rendered voxels always get drawn on top of the distance-optimized fake voxels."

animation curve looks kind of scary to work with I think I have a way that I can simplify this for you and make it click instantly so I made this ball animation here where there is a key frame at the bottom a key frame at the top and a key frame at the bottom again right and it just basically loops around now this ball animation is kind of bad if we look at it cuz it just kind of like hovers up and down it doesn't really have that real ball motion where it lingers a little bit in the air and it quickly bounces off the ground right so if I select this middle one here and I press control tab you can see I go into the curve editor and I have now selected that one key frame that we selected out here so what I like to do before we do anything particular is that over here I want to set it to individual centers cuz if I don't you can see here if I scale my curve it's just going to scale it all and looks really weird but if we set it to that individual centers and I select the middle key frame here you can see I just scaled that individual key frame there so you can see if I scale this up it already kind of looks a little bit like we have that motion of a ball where goes down bounces it up lingers in the air and it goes down again lingers go down up it still feels a little flat when it hits the ground so if we go back out here and I press C and I just paint over these and I paint over these control tap back in and I can then scale this down you can see now you can really see that curve that a ball usually have when it bounces so let's try and play see how this looks so it lingers bounces off lingers bounces off and I think this is like the most simple way that I can EXP explain an animation curve so essentially if we scale a curve up it lingers longer on that key frame and if we scale it down it quickly moves on to the next key frame and this is sort of the way that made it kind of Click for me
